## Source(s) and Extraction

```{python}
# Required packages — run this once in the terminal before using the notebook:
# pip install nflreadpy pandas pyarrow

import nflreadpy as nfl
import pandas as pd
```

---

### Source Details Documentation

For this project, we are using NFL play-by-play data from the nflverse through the Python package nflreadpy. We chose play-by-play data because our project is focused on team tendencies in specific situations (like early downs, fourth downs, and red zone plays), and this dataset has one row per play, which makes that kind of analysis possible.
**How to find and access the same data:**

The package is called `nflreadpy` and can be installed from PyPI. The package homepage is at [https://nflreadpy.nflverse.com/](https://nflreadpy.nflverse.com/), and the source code is on GitHub at [https://github.com/nflverse/nflreadpy](https://github.com/nflverse/nflreadpy). To install it, run:

```bash
pip install nflreadpy pandas pyarrow
```

Once installed, the 2023 play-by-play data can be loaded with one function call:

```python
pbp = nfl.load_pbp(seasons=[2023])
```

The full API reference for available load functions is at [https://nflreadpy.nflverse.com/api/load_functions/](https://nflreadpy.nflverse.com/api/load_functions/). Data dictionaries describing every column in the play-by-play dataset are available through the related R package's documentation at [https://nflreadr.nflverse.com/articles/dictionary_pbp.html](https://nflreadr.nflverse.com/articles/dictionary_pbp.html). The raw data files themselves are hosted on GitHub under [https://github.com/nflverse/nflverse-data](https://github.com/nflverse/nflverse-data), where `nflreadpy` retrieves them as Parquet files.

**How the data was originally collected and generated:**

The data comes from official NFL game records (along with sources like ESPN), where every play in every game is logged in real time. The nflverse team processes that raw data using tools like nflfastR, adding extra fields like expected points, win probability, and other advanced metrics.

After that, the cleaned and enhanced data is uploaded to GitHub and updated regularly during the season. The nflreadpy package just makes it easy to download and load that data into Python.
**Variables of interest:**

Below are the main variables we will use. Some of these come directly from the dataset, and a few will be created later. These variables were selcted as they support analytical goals of the project like fourth down decision making, measuring passing tendencies, scoring efficiency, and red-zone touchdown effectiveness. 
| Variable Name | Description | Values |
|---|---|---|
| `season` | The NFL season year | Integer; `2023` for this project |
| `week` | The week number within the season | Integer; 1–18 for regular season games |
| `game_id` | A unique identifier for each game | String; formatted as `YYYY_WW_AWAY_HOME`, e.g. `2023_01_BUF_NYJ` |
| `season_type` | Whether the game was regular season or postseason | String; `"REG"` or `"POST"` — we filter to `"REG"` only |
| `posteam` | The team on offense (the team with possession) | String; 2–3 letter NFL team abbreviation, e.g. `"KC"` or `"PHI"` |
| `defteam` | The team on defense | String; same abbreviation format as `posteam` |
| `down` | The current down | Integer; 1, 2, 3, or 4; `NaN` on kickoffs and other non-scrimmage plays |
| `ydstogo` | Yards needed to earn a first down | Integer; typically 1–99; on 4th down this is also yards needed for a conversion |
| `yardline_100` | Yards from the offensive team's current position to the opponent's end zone | Integer; 1 means the offense is at the opponent's 1-yard line, 99 means they are at their own 1-yard line |
| `play_type` | The category of play | String; values include `"pass"`, `"run"`, `"punt"`, `"field_goal"`, `"kickoff"`, `"no_play"`, `"qb_kneel"`, `"qb_spike"` |
| `pass` | Whether the play was a pass attempt (includes plays that ended in a sack) | Binary integer; `1` = pass, `0` = not a pass; `NaN` on non-scrimmage plays |
| `rush` | Whether the play was a rushing attempt | Binary integer; `1` = rush, `0` = not a rush; `NaN` on non-scrimmage plays |
| `first_down` | Whether the play resulted in a first down being earned | Binary integer; `1` = first down earned, `0` = no first down |
| `touchdown` | Whether the play resulted in a touchdown | Binary integer; `1` = touchdown scored, `0` = no touchdown |
| `fourth_down_converted` | Whether the offense successfully converted a 4th down attempt | Binary integer; `1` = converted, `0` = failed; `NaN` when the play was not a 4th down attempt |
| `fourth_down_failed` | Whether the offense failed to convert a 4th down attempt | Binary integer; `1` = failed, `0` = converted or not applicable; `NaN` when not a 4th down attempt |
| `drive` | The drive number within the game for the offensive team | Integer; resets to 1 at the start of each game |
| `drive_play_count` | The total number of plays that occurred during the current drive | Integer |
| `drive_ended_with_score` | Whether the drive ended with a touchdown or field goal | Binary integer; `1` = scoring drive, `0` = drive did not end in a score |
| `yards_gained` | The number of yards gained (or lost) on the play | Integer; can be negative for losses on the play |
| `game_seconds_remaining` | Total seconds left in the game at the time of the play | Integer; ranges from 3600 (start of game) to 0 (end of game) |
| `score_differential` | The offensive team's current score minus the defensive team's score | Integer; negative values mean the offense is trailing |
| `in_red_zone` | *(Derived)* Whether the offense's line of scrimmage is inside the opponent's 20-yard line | Binary integer; `1` if `yardline_100 <= 20`, otherwise `0` |

---

### Extraction Code

```{python}
# Load 2023 NFL play-by-play data for the regular season
pbp = nfl.load_pbp(seasons=[2023])

# nflreadpy loads data as a Polars DataFrame by default.
# We convert it to pandas since our group is more comfortable
# using pandas for filtering, grouping, and exporting.
pbp = pbp.to_pandas()

# Confirm the data loaded correctly
print("Shape (rows, columns):", pbp.shape)
print("\nFirst 20 column names:")
print(list(pbp.columns[:20]))
```

---

### Preview of Raw Dataset

```{python}
pbp.head()
```

---

### Save Raw Dataset

```{python}
# Save the raw extracted data before any transformations
pbp.to_csv("raw_nfl_pbp_2023.csv", index=False)
print("Saved: raw_nfl_pbp_2023.csv")
```

---

### Extraction Code Documentation

The load_pbp() function pulls the 2023 play-by-play data directly from nflverse. The data first loads as a Polars DataFrame, so we convert it to pandas because that’s what we’ve been using in class.

We only load one season to keep things consistent and easier to analyze. After loading, the dataset has around 49,000 plays, and each row represents one play.

At this point, the dataset still includes everything (like kickoffs and penalties), so we’ll filter it down later based on what we actually need for our questions.
